behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from recommendation results regardless of their relevance score
behaviors.justification: Real failure occurs when the filter checks the wrong field in product data or when the actual product API schema differs from test expectations. A unit mock with test data won't catch schema mismatches (e.g., if real products use 'stock_quantity' instead of 'is_in_stock'). Integration test against actual product catalog structure is required to verify the filter works with real data semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Load product database with mix of in-stock and out-of-stock items, request recommendations, and verify that all out-of-stock products are excluded from results regardless of their algorithmic score.

behaviors.description: User preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings
behaviors.justification: Real failure occurs when user settings schema or category representation differs from assumptions. Unit mock of settings won't expose structural differences in actual user preference data (e.g., categories stored as IDs vs strings, nested vs flat). Integration test with real user settings schema is needed to verify the filter works correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with several categories marked as blocked in real settings storage, request recommendations, and verify all products from blocked categories are excluded from results.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products matching recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal
behaviors.justification: Unit mock with synthetic browsing data won't expose failures when real browsing history structure differs (e.g., how timestamps are stored, how categories are tagged in history). The ranking algorithm's correctness depends on correctly extracting and weighting real browsing data. Integration test with realistic browsing patterns is needed to verify the algorithm applies correct weights.
behaviors.test_description: Create realistic user browsing history in a subset of product categories with varying recency, request recommendations, and verify that products from recently browsed categories appear higher in rankings than products with no browsing signal.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed
behaviors.justification: Non-deterministic ordering can hide in real database queries even when unit-level logic is correct. Database result ordering depends on query plan, index selection, and internal state—issues invisible in unit tests. Unit mock won't expose real DB-level ordering instability (e.g., hash-based iteration, concurrent updates). Integration test against actual database needed to catch order instability across pagination calls.
behaviors.test_description: Request page 2 of recommendations twice in immediate succession with no intervening data changes, and verify the exact same products appear in the same order both times.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding filter bubble
behaviors.justification: The serendipity logic must correctly identify categories the user hasn't browsed and find products from them. Unit mock with simplified category taxonomy won't verify the algorithm handles real category structure (hierarchy, aliases, metadata). Real failures occur when category definitions differ from assumptions. Integration test with actual category taxonomy and real browsing history needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with browsing history limited to a small subset of product categories (e.g., 3 of 10), request recommendations, and verify that at least one result comes from a category the user has not previously browsed.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set
behaviors.justification: While the fallback logic itself could be tested at Unit level, the real failure is when popularity data is missing, empty, or in wrong format. Unit mock of popularity might not catch calculation bugs or data availability issues. Integration test with real popularity scores is needed to ensure the fallback path actually returns valid recommendations.
behaviors.test_description: Create a brand-new user with zero purchase history and zero browsing history, request recommendations, and verify a non-empty list of popular products is returned (not an error or empty result set).

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with
behaviors.justification: Real failure occurs when tracking system schema differs from test expectations (field names, types, timestamp format). Unit mock might use wrong event structure or omit required fields. The tracking system's storage and retrieval must be verified—field names or formats might differ from assumptions. Integration test with actual tracking system needed.
behaviors.test_description: Simulate a user clicking a recommendation, query the tracking system, and verify the recorded event contains the correct product ID, correct position in the original list (with correct indexing), and a valid timestamp within the correct time window.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products
behaviors.justification: Performance characteristics are completely invisible at Unit level. Real failure occurs when algorithm complexity doesn't scale (e.g., O(n²) with 100k products). Integration test with scaled database (100k+ rows) is minimum to expose algorithmic complexity. System test with production-like infrastructure (indexing, caching, network) might reveal additional issues, but Integration catches the fundamental algorithmic failure.
behaviors.test_description: Load product database with 100,000 items, execute recommendation requests for varied user profiles (new user, browsing history, purchase history), and measure E2E latency distribution. Assert that p99 latency stays under 200ms across multiple runs.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests
behaviors.justification: State persistence across multiple requests cannot be tested at Unit level. Real failure occurs when purchase state doesn't propagate to ranking logic, or when ranking state isn't updated after a purchase. Unit mock can't verify that ranking correctly reflects purchase history across requests. Integration test needed to verify purchase state is persisted and correctly affects ranking on subsequent calls.
behaviors.test_description: Request recommendations for a user, identify the top-ranked product, simulate a purchase of that product, request recommendations again immediately, and verify that the purchased product ranks lower (or is absent) in the new results.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list
behaviors.justification: Non-determinism can arise from unordered data structures (hash iteration), concurrent updates, or floating-point precision—all invisible in Unit tests with mocked data. Real failure occurs when ordering differs between identical requests due to database state or concurrent access. Unit mock won't expose these issues. Integration test against real database with potential concurrent access needed to catch non-determinism.
behaviors.test_description: Request recommendations twice in rapid succession with no intervening user activity (no clicks, no purchases, no time passage), retrieve both result sets, and verify they are byte-identical (same products in same order).
